tagline: rpg ships explain plan sharing; sqlever adds static analysis; one API key learns a hard lesson.
editionNote: Three major releases, 87 commits across rpg, plus analysis rules landing in sqlever.

articles.headline: rpg v0.8.0 brings EXPLAIN plan visualization and one-command sharing to three platforms
articles.deck: *Colorized plans with automatic issue detection; export to explain.depesz.com, dalibo, or pgMustard in a single command.*
articles.body: @NikolayS merged #696 shipping v0.8.0 on March 20. The release adds a suite of EXPLAIN enhancements: summary headers showing execution time, planning time, row counts, and buffer stats; automatic warnings for seq scans on large tables, row estimate errors >10x, spills to disk, and parallel worker failures; and full colorization by time percentage with a bolded hot path. The new '/explain share' commands (#670) push plans directly to explain.depesz.com, dalibo.com, or pgMustard without leaving the terminal. Lua custom commands are now supported via '--features lua', letting users define '/' commands like '/slow_mean' and '/slow_total'. Tab completion now covers all rpg backslash commands.
articles.illustrationPrompt: a database query execution plan tree displayed on a terminal screen, nodes highlighted by color intensity.

articles.headline: pgMustard API key exposed in PR comment #670 during feature testing
articles.deck: *Caught and rotated quickly—a reminder that AI agents posting test output need careful handling.*
articles.body: During testing of the new '/explain share pgmustard' feature in rpg v0.8.0, an AI agent posted test output containing a pgMustard API key directly into PR comment #670. @NikolayS spotted it, rotated the key immediately, and no downstream access was detected. The incident is a clean example of the friction between automated testing and secret hygiene: feature work that touches external APIs should gate AI-generated output through review before it reaches version control.
articles.illustrationPrompt: a padlock resting on a stack of disk drives, slightly ajar.

articles.headline: rpg v0.7.0 wraps '/ask' queries in read-only transactions for database-level safety
articles.deck: *Every AI-generated query now executes inside 'start transaction read only', blocking writes even if classification fails.*
articles.body: @NikolayS released v0.7.0 on March 19, hardening the '/ask' AI query execution path. All queries from the AI assistant now execute inside 'start transaction read only' (#626, #634), providing database-level protection against accidental mutations—the read-only transaction guard stays even if the query classifier misbehaves. Write queries in '/ask' mode are explicitly refused (#631); if users need writes via text2sql, they must use '/t2s' and confirm explicitly. Internal '/ask' transactions auto-rollback on error or cancellation (#639), preventing idle-in-tx hangs. Ctrl-C now aborts LLM requests mid-flight (#641), and 'ai.timeout' (default 30s) prevents hanging on unresponsive providers (#640).
articles.illustrationPrompt: a transaction lock icon with a barrier crossing it, preventing passage.

articles.headline: rpg v0.5.0 auto-detects AI provider from environment and adds multi-category syntax highlighting
articles.deck: *No more manual provider configuration; keywords, strings, operators, and error prefixes now in distinct colors.*
articles.body: @NikolayS released v0.5.0 on March 17, streamlining setup and visual parsing. The terminal now auto-detects ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, OPENAI_API_KEY, or OLLAMA_API_KEY from the environment, eliminating manual provider configuration. Multi-category SQL syntax highlighting now separates keywords, strings, and operators into distinct colors; error/warning/notice prefixes are colorized (red/yellow/cyan). Ctrl-C now cancels the '/fix' execute prompt without running the query. The codebase shed autonomous agent references (AAA, O/S/A) to reflect rpg's focus as a focused terminal tool with light AI integration.
articles.illustrationPrompt: colorful text on a dark terminal screen, syntax keywords highlighted in separate hues.

articles.headline: sqlever adds 20 static analysis rules for DDL, DML, and locking safety
articles.deck: *SA001–SA010 cover DDL safety; SA011–SA021 cover DML, concurrency, and locking hazards.*
articles.body: @NikolayS merged analysis rules in two waves: #65 landing SA001–SA010 (DDL safety checks) and #72 adding SA011–SA021 (DML, DDL, and locking safety). The analysis engine itself landed in #64, providing the rule registry, preprocessor, and suppression framework. @NikolayS also shipped the standalone 'sqlever analyze' command (#70) and 'sqlever verify' to run verification scripts after deploy (#69). Together with the deploy command (#63) and status display (#62), sqlever now has a functional analysis and deployment pipeline for zero-downtime migrations.
articles.illustrationPrompt: a pair of hands examining a document with a magnifying glass, marking errors.

articles.headline: pg_ash adds security policy and configures dependabot for CI
articles.deck: *SECURITY.md published; GitHub Actions versions now auto-updated by dependabot.*
articles.body: @NikolayS merged #24 adding a SECURITY.md policy and #23 configuring dependabot.yml to keep GitHub Actions versions current. The Active Session History extension is now actively maintained with security and dependency hygiene in place.
articles.illustrationPrompt: a shield with a checkmark symbol on a stone foundation.

articles.headline: oak-tree-buzzer v1.3.1 and v1.2.5 debug builds available
articles.deck: *Direct Android tablet installation; version now visible in bottom-right corner.*
articles.body: Two debug builds shipped March 17 and March 15 for the LAN-only dental office buzzer system. Version display is now visible in the app UI.
articles.illustrationPrompt: an Android tablet screen displaying a version number in the corner.

closingNote: Next week: sqlever's real-world integration tests. Submit your migrations. 🔧
